article:
Chunk # 0.
Mouse Liver Mitochondria Isolation, Size Fractionation, and 
Real-time MOMP Measurement
Thibaud T Renault1,2,3,4,5,6,*, Mark P.A Luna-Vargas1,2, and Jerry E Chipuk1,2,3,4,7,*
1Department of Oncological Sciences, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, One Gustave L 
Levy Place, New Y ork, USA
2The Tisch Cancer Institute, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, One Gustave L Levy 
Place, New Y ork, USA
3The Diabetes, Obesity, and Metabolism Institute, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, One 
Gustave L Levy Place, New Y ork, USA
4Department of Dermatology, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, One Gustave L Levy 
Place, New Y ork, USA
5Helmholtz Centre for Infection Research, Braunschweig, Germany
6Max Planck Institute for Infection Biology, Charitéplatz 1, Campus Charité Mitte, Berlin, Germany
7The Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, One 
Gustave L Levy Place, New Y ork, USA
Abstract
The mitochondrial pathway of apoptosis involves a complex interplay between dozens of proteins 
and lipids, and is also dependent on the shape and size of mitochondria The use of cellular models 
in past studies has not been ideal for investigating how the complex multi-factor interplay 
regulates the molecular mechanisms of mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP) 
Isolated systems have proven to be a paradigm to deconstruct MOMP into individual steps and to 
study the behavior of each subset of MOMP regulators In particular, isolated mitochondria are 
key to in vitro  studies of the BCL-2 family proteins, a complex family of pro-survival and pro-
apoptotic proteins that directly control the mitochondrial pathway of apoptosis ( Renault et al., 
2013 ) In this protocol, we describe three complementary procedures for investigating in real-time the 
effects of MOMP regulators using isolated mitochondria The first procedure is “Liver 
mitochondria isolation” in which the liver is dissected from mice to obtain mitochondria 
“Mitochondria labeling with JC-1 and size fractionation” is the second procedure that describes a 
method to label, fractionate by size and standardize subpopulations of mitochondria Finally, the 
“Real-time MOMP measurements” protocol allows to follow MOMP in real-time on isolated 
mitochondria The aforementioned procedures were used to determine in vitro  the role of 
mitochondrial membrane shape at the level of isolated cells and isolated mitochondria ( Renault et 
al., 2015 ) *For correspondence: thibaud.renault@helmholtz-hzi.de; jerry.chipuk@mssm.edu 
HHS Public Access
Author manuscript

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:**

1. Dissect the liver from C57BL/6 mice.
2. Place the liver in a 50 ml conical centrifuge tube.
3. Homogenize the liver using a 15 ml Potter-Elvehjem dounce homogenizer.
4. Centrifuge the homogenate at 600 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
5. Collect the supernatant and transfer it to a new 15 ml conical centrifuge tube.
6. Centrifuge the supernatant at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
7. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
8. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
9. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
10. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
11. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
12. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
13. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.

15. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
16. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
17. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
18. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
19. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
20. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
21. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.

23. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
24. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
25. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
26. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
27. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
28. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
29. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
30. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
31. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
32. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
33. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.
34. Centrifuge the resuspended pellet at 12,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C.
35. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in trehalose isolation buffer.

summary:
Summary 4: Mouse Liver Mitochondria Isolation, Size Fractionation, and Real-time MOMP Measurement

Thibaud T Renault, Mark P.A Luna-Vargas, and Jerry E Chipuk conducted a study on the mitochondrial pathway of apoptosis at the Icahn School of Medicine in New York. Collaborating with the Helmholtz Centre for Infection Research and the Max Planck Institute for Infection Biology in Berlin, along with various departments at the Icahn School of Medicine, they aimed to understand the complex interplay and molecular mechanisms of mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP).

The researchers used three procedures: liver mitochondria isolation, mitochondria labeling with JC-1 and size fractionation, and real-time MOMP measurements. These methods allowed them to investigate the role of BCL-2 family proteins, pro-survival and pro-apoptotic proteins, in regulating MOMP. They also studied the impact of mitochondrial membrane shape on MOMP.

By deconstructing MOMP into individual steps and studying the behavior of MOMP regulators, the researchers gained insights into the multi-factor interplay involved in this process. The findings contribute to our understanding of the mitochondrial pathway of apoptosis and its regulation in isolated systems.

article:
Chunk # 1.
Author manuscript; available in PMC 2017 February 05 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author ManuscriptProcedure
A Liver mitochondria isolation
Animal handling, euthanasia, and dissection must be done following the Institutional Animal 
Care and Use Committee guidelines We have successfully utilized carbon dioxide 
asphyxiation before cervical dislocation to euthanize the mice However, we advise the 
reader to consult and follow the Animal Care and Use guidelines of their own institute for 
the appropriate procedure Following steps involved in animal handling and dissection are 
executed wearing gloves 1 Visualize the liver, the bile duct, and gallbladder The bile 
duct and gallbladder must be removed or kept separate 
from the liver to avoid contamination of the mitochondria 
with bile (Figure 1a–c) 2 Excise the four liver lobes and transfer immediately to a 50 
ml conical tube containing ice-cold PBS In case of blood 
contamination, soak the liver in ice-cold PBS until no more 
blood is washed out (Figure 1d–e) Note: From this point, all the steps must be performed 
using reagents and materials at 4 °C to minimize the 
activation of proteases and phospholipases 3 Transfer the liver with forceps to a clean Petri dish placed 
on ice and drain off the excess PBS 4 Mince the liver using a clean razor blade until forming a 
homogeneous paste (Figure 2) 5 Transfer half of the liver paste using the razor blade as a 
spoon into a chilled Potter-Elvehjem dounce homogenizer 
containing 10 ml TIB 6 Insert the pestle into the homogenizer and gently push it 
downwards Early resistance is common during the first 
strokes, and no excessive force should be applied to the 
liver paste Instead, move the pestle upwards to resuspend 
the liver paste and slowly repeat those steps until all the 
material is able to go past the pestle without strong 
resistance The homogenization step can be done on the 
bench as long the whole procedure is done quickly enough 
to maintain the temperature close to 4 °C 7 Homogenize the paste five times, pour into a 15 ml conical 
tube and store on ice until all the liver paste is processed 
(Figure 2) 8 Repeat steps 6–8 with the second half of the liver paste.Renault et al Page 4
Bio Protoc  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2017 February 05 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript9 The following differential centrifugation steps are required 
to obtain the mitochondrial fraction All the steps should be 
performed in a swinging bucket centrifuge at 4 °C 
Resuspension of pellets is done gently by pipetting using a 
P1000 pipette (Figure 2) a 600 x g for 10 min Transfer 
the supernatant (S/N) to a 
clean, pre-chilled 15 ml 
conical tube b 3,500 x g for 10 min 
Discard the S/N Resuspend 
the pellet in 10 ml TIB c 1,500 x g for 5 min Transfer 
the S/N to a clean, pre-
chilled 15 ml conical tube d 5,500 x g for 10 min 
Discard the S/N Resuspend 
the pellet in 10 ml TIB e Repeat steps A9b to A9d, 
but resuspend the final pellet 
in 500 μl TIB 10 To quantify the amount of isolated material in the 
resuspended pellet (step A9e), add 5 μl of the resuspended 
pellet to 995 μl of TIB (1:200 dilution) and measure the 
OD520 with a spectrophotometer Dilute the mitochondria 
with TIB to standardize the sample to an OD520 value of 
0.25 (~20 μg/μl of protein) 11 At this point the isolated mitochondria can be aliquoted (50 
μl), frozen in dry ice-ethanol to prevent formation of water 
crystals, and stored at −80 °C Note: Freshly isolated mitochondria from the previous step 
should be kept on ice and used within 2 h to ensure 
integrity of the outer membrane If frozen mitochondria are 
used, thaw the sample in a 30 °C water bath and continue 
using TIB in the subsequent procedures B Mitochondria labeling with JC-1 and size fractionation
Labeling mitochondria with JC-1, a potential-dependent mitochondrial dye that changes 
color as membrane potentials increase during MOMP, allows to standardize the 
mitochondria fractions and to record MOMP in real-time (as example, see Renault et al., 
2015 , Figure 4F) The labeling and fractionation step offers the opportunity to select the Renault et al Page 5
Bio Protoc  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2017 February 05 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptmitochondria by size: big mitochondria will be eluted prior to smaller ones This 
fractionation step is optional and requires to calibrate the column using liposomes of a 
known size as a standard Another option is to determine the average mitochondrial size in 
each fraction a posteriori,  using for example dynamic light scattering If fractionation by size 
is not desired, all the mitochondria-containing fractions can be combined in step B8 1

mitochondria.protocol:
Liver mitochondria isolation involves the following steps:

1. Visualize the liver, bile duct, and gallbladder. Remove or keep the bile duct and gallbladder separate from the liver to avoid contamination of the mitochondria with bile.
2. Excise the four liver lobes and transfer them immediately to a 50 ml conical tube containing ice-cold PBS. If there is blood contamination, soak the liver in ice-cold PBS until no more blood is washed out.
3. Transfer the liver to a clean Petri dish placed on ice and drain off the excess PBS.
4. Mince the liver using a clean razor blade until a homogeneous paste is formed.
5. Transfer half of the liver paste to a chilled Potter-Elvehjem dounce homogenizer containing 10 ml TIB.
6. Insert the pestle into the homogenizer and gently push it downwards. Move the pestle upwards to resuspend the liver paste and repeat these steps until all the material can go past the pestle without strong resistance. The homogenization step can be done on the bench as long as the whole procedure is done quickly enough to maintain a temperature close to 4 °C.
7. Homogenize the paste five times, pour it into a 15 ml conical tube, and store it on ice until all the liver paste is processed.
8. Repeat steps 5-7 with the second half of the liver paste.
9. Perform the following differential centrifugation steps in a swinging bucket centrifuge at 4 °C:
   a. Centrifuge at 600 x g for 10 min. Transfer the supernatant to a clean, pre-chilled 15 ml conical tube.
   b. Centrifuge at 3,500 x g for 10 min. Discard the supernatant. Resuspend the pellet in 10 ml TIB.
   c. Centrifuge at 1,500 x g for 5 min. Transfer the supernatant to a clean, pre-chilled 15 ml conical tube.
   d. Centrifuge at 5,500 x g for 10 min. Discard the supernatant. Resuspend the pellet in 10 ml TIB.
   e. Repeat steps 9b to 9d, but resuspend the final pellet in 500 μl TIB.
10. To quantify the amount of isolated material in the resuspended pellet from step 9e, add 5 μl of the resuspended pellet to 995 μl of TIB (1:200 dilution) and measure the OD520 with a spectrophotometer. Dilute the mitochondria with TIB to standardize the sample to an OD520 value of 0.25 (~20 μg/μl of protein).
11. Aliquot the isolated mitochondria (50 μl), freeze them in dry ice-ethanol to prevent the formation of water crystals, and store them at -80 °C. Freshly isolated mitochondria should be used within 2 hours to ensure the integrity of the outer membrane. If frozen mitochondria are used, thaw the sample in a 30 °C water bath and continue using TIB in subsequent procedures.

In terms of protocol completeness, the article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in liver mitochondria isolation. However, there are some gaps in the protocol. The article does not provide information on the specific composition or preparation of the ice-cold PBS, TIB, or the 50 ml conical tube. Additionally, the article does not mention the duration of each centrifugation step or the speed settings for the swinging bucket centrifuge. These missing details could be important for accurately replicating the protocol.

summary:
Revised Summary (Iteration 1): To isolate liver mitochondria, follow these steps:
1. Euthanize mice using carbon dioxide asphyxiation before cervical dislocation, following animal care guidelines.
2. Visualize and separate the liver, bile duct, and gallbladder to avoid contamination.
3. Transfer the liver lobes to ice-cold PBS and remove any blood contamination.
4. Mince the liver to form a homogeneous paste.
5. Transfer half of the paste to a dounce homogenizer with TIB solution and gently homogenize.
6. Repeat the homogenization step and store the homogenized paste on ice.
7. Perform sequential centrifugation steps at 4 °C to obtain the mitochondrial fraction and resuspend the pellets gently.
8. Measure the amount of isolated material in the resuspended pellet to quantify the isolated material.
9. Keep freshly isolated mitochondria on ice and use within 2 hours to maintain outer membrane integrity.

Missing Entities: The removal of the bile duct and gallbladder to avoid contamination, differential centrifugation to obtain the mitochondrial fraction, and the need to follow animal care guidelines throughout the process.

article:
Chunk # 2.
Dilute 50 μl of mitochondria in 250 μl TIB buffer 2 Add JC-1 (200 μM) to the diluted mitochondria to a final 
concentration of 15 μM and incubate for 10 min at 30 °C 
(Figure 3) 3 To remove unbound JC-1, centrifuge the mitochondria at 
5,550 x g for 10 min at room temperature and resuspend the 
pellet in 25 μl TIB 4 Pre-equilibrate a 2 ml CL-2B gravity-column with 2 
column volumes of TIB (4 ml) using gravity to let the TIB 
to flow through the CL-2B resin, load the resuspended JC-1 
mitochondria onto the column, and allow sample to flow 
through Note: Make sure the resin does not run dry before loading 
the JC-1 labeled mitochondria 5 Slowly apply 4 column volumes of TIB (4 × 2 ml) to the 
column using a clean Pasteur pipet and collect 20 × 100 μl 
(~2–3 drops) fractions in micro-centrifuge tubes 6 To determine which fractions contain labeled mitochondria, 
pre-fill a 96-well plate with 95 μl of 0.1% Triton X-100 in 
water (0.1% Triton X-100 permeabilizes mitochondria) and 
add 5 μl sample of each fraction to a separate well 7 Measure the fluorescence using a spectrophotometer (Ex: 
561 nm/Em: 620 nm) to identify the mitochondria-
containing fractions (typical values are 10,000–50,000 
Relative Fluorescence Units for big mitochondria, and > 
500 RFU for small size mitochondria) 8 Combine the desired fractions containing JC-1 labeled 
mitochondria and standardize the samples using relative 
JC-1 fluorescence intensity (determine the fluorescence of 
each combined sample as described in step B6-7 and dilute 
the most concentrated samples with TIB buffer to reach the 
concentration of the lowest sample) Note: In our experience 0.05–1 μm liposomes were 
subjected onto the CL-2B gravity-column and used as 
reference for selecting appropriate fractions Briefly, 
fluorescent liposomes of several sizes (0.05, 0.2, and 1 μm) Renault et al Page 6
Bio Protoc  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2017 February 05 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptwere prepared according to Asciolla et al., 2012 The 
liposomes were loaded onto the CL-2B gravity column and 
100 μl fractions were collected, as described in steps B4-6 
of this protocol Identification of liposome-containing 
fractions for each liposome size allows to determine in 
which fractions vesicles of a given size should be expected C Real-time MOMP measurements
Real-time MOMP quantification is determined by measuring the mitochondrial membrane 
potential (Δ ΨM) using the fluorescent JC-1 dye The JC-1 dye exhibits potential-dependent 
accumulation in mitochondria, indicated by a fluorescence emission shift from green (~529 
nm) to red (~590 nm) MOMP and the subsequent loss of Δ ΨM are indicated by a decrease 
in the red/green fluorescence intensity ratio There is a plethora of options to investigate a 
subset of inducers and regulators of MOMP in real-time It is important to have internal and 
experimental controls An example is given in Table 1 For the positive internal control, we 
use FCCP that uncouples the electron transport from oxidative phosphorylation in 
mitochondria and completely depolarizes the mitochondrial membrane To generate a 
positive experimental control, we use detergent-activated recombinant BAX ( Hsu and Youle, 
1997 ) The detergent, β-octylglucoside (OG), artificially triggers BAX activation, and 
therefore BAXOG (20–50 nM) can be used as a reliable positive control (for 100 μl of 2.3 
μM BAXOG: 5 μg BAX + 0.7% OG in liposome buffer, incubate for 60 min at 4 °C, aliquot 
and store at −80 °C) Real-time measurements
1 Prepare a 2x proteins/inducers solution in TIB and dispense 
50 μl in each well in a 96-well plate The plate should 
contain the following internal and experimental controls 
(Figure 4; Table 1)
a Negative internal control: 
TIB only
b Positive internal control: 
FCCP (10 μM final 
concentration)
c Negative experimental 
control: purified 
recombinant BAX (20–50 
nM final concentration)
d Positive experimental 
control: β-octylglucoside-
activated BAX (BAXOG, 20–
50 nM final concentration)Renault et al Page 7
Bio Protoc  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2017 February 05 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript2 Prepare a 2x suspension of JC-1 loaded mitochondria (~2.5 
RFU/μl) and add 50 μl to each well to reach a final 
concentration of 1.25 RFU/μl Note: ~50–100 RFUs per reaction is ideal 3 Measure fluorescence every 5 min in a plate reader for 60 
min (Ex: 561 nm/Em: 620 nm) at 37 °C 4 Calculate the percentage of mitochondria that have 
undergone MOMP using the following formula:
Where RFU buffer  and RFU FCCP  represent the fluorescence 
of the negative and positive internal controls, respectively, 
and RFU sample  the fluorescence of the sample (see Figure 
4G–H as example in Renault et al., 2015 ) Recipes
1

mitochondria.protocol:
Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:
1. Dilute 50 μl of mitochondria in 250 μl TIB buffer.
2. Add JC-1 (200 μM) to the diluted mitochondria to a final concentration of 15 μM and incubate for 10 min at 30 °C.
3. To remove unbound JC-1, centrifuge the mitochondria at 5,550 x g for 10 min at room temperature and resuspend the pellet in 25 μl TIB.
4. Pre-equilibrate a 2 ml CL-2B gravity-column with 2 column volumes of TIB (4 ml) using gravity to let the TIB flow through the CL-2B resin. Load the resuspended JC-1 mitochondria onto the column and allow the sample to flow through. Note: Make sure the resin does not run dry before loading the JC-1 labeled mitochondria.
5. Slowly apply 4 column volumes of TIB (4 × 2 ml) to the column using a clean Pasteur pipet and collect 20 × 100 μl (~2–3 drops) fractions in micro-centrifuge tubes.
6. To determine which fractions contain labeled mitochondria, pre-fill a 96-well plate with 95 μl of 0.1% Triton X-100 in water and add 5 μl sample of each fraction to a separate well.
7. Measure the fluorescence using a spectrophotometer (Ex: 561 nm/Em: 620 nm) to identify the mitochondria-containing fractions. Typical values are 10,000–50,000 Relative Fluorescence Units for big mitochondria, and > 500 RFU for small size mitochondria.
8. Combine the desired fractions containing JC-1 labeled mitochondria and standardize the samples using relative JC-1 fluorescence intensity. Determine the fluorescence of each combined sample and dilute the most concentrated samples with TIB buffer to reach the concentration of the lowest sample. Note: 0.05–1 μm liposomes were subjected onto the CL-2B gravity-column and used as a reference for selecting appropriate fractions.

Conclusion:
The protocol for mitochondrial isolation described in the article is mostly complete. It includes the dilution of mitochondria, addition of JC-1 dye, centrifugation, column chromatography, fraction collection, fluorescence measurement, and sample standardization. However, there are a few gaps in the protocol. The article does not provide details on the preparation of liposomes used as a reference, and it does not specify the composition of the TIB buffer. Additionally, the protocol does not mention the volume of the fractions collected during column chromatography. These missing details should be obtained from other sources or the authors.
mitochondria.organisms:
Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:
No organisms mentioned.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation according to Renault et al:

1. Prepare Trehalose isolation buffer (TIB) with the following components:
   - 200 mM trehalose
   - 68 mM sucrose
   - 10 mM HEPES-KOH, pH 7.4
   - 10 mM KCl
   - 1 mM EDTA
   - 1 mM EGTA
   - 0.1% BSA
   - Protease inhibitors cocktail (freshly added according to manufacturer's instructions)

2. Prepare JC-1 loading buffer:
   - Prepare a 200 μM stock solution in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO)
   - Dilute the stock solution accordingly in TIB

3. Prepare Liposome buffer with the following components:
   - 0.2 mM EDTA
   - 10 mM HEPES-KOH, pH 7.4
   - 200 mM KCl
   - 5 mM MgCl2

Note: The article does not provide complete details for the mitochondrial isolation protocol. It only mentions the preparation of TIB, JC-1 loading buffer, and Liposome buffer. The specific steps for the isolation procedure are not provided.

summary:
Summary 4: SPR:

1. Trehalose isolation buffer (TIB) is prepared using BSA-Fraction V to eliminate fatty acid and lipid contaminants that promote non-specific BAK/BAX activation. It contains 200 mM trehalose, 68 mM sucrose, 10 mM HEPES-KOH (pH 7.4), 10 mM KCl, 1 mM EDTA, 1 mM EGTA, and 0.1% BSA. Protease inhibitors cocktail should be added according to the manufacturer's instructions.

2. JC-1 loading buffer, prepared in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and diluted in TIB, is used for mitochondrial labeling.

3. Liposome buffer contains 0.2 mM EDTA, 10 mM HEPES-KOH (pH 7.4), 200 mM KCl, and 5 mM MgCl2.

4. The Chipuk Laboratory, supported by NIH grants CA157740 and CA206005, conducted mouse dissection, isolated liver mitochondria, performed mitochondrial labeling and size fractionation, and studied Real-Time MOMP with internal and experimental controls. The project is also supported by a pilot project from NIH P20AA017067, the JJR Foundation, the William A Spivak Fund, the Fridolin Charitable Trust, an American Cancer Society Research Scholar Award, a Leukemia and Lymphoma Scholar Award, and the Developmental Research Pilot Project Program within the Department of Oncological Sciences at Mount Sinai.

5. DMSO is used in the pilot project from NIH P20AA017067 for liver mitochondria isolation.

6. The Chipuk Laboratory provides assistance and support.

7. References: 
- Asciolla JJ, Renault TT, Chipuk JE. Examining BCL-2 family function with large unilamellar vesicles. J Vis Exp 2012; (68).
- Hsu YT, Youle RJ. Nonionic detergents induce dimerization among members of the Bcl-2 family. J Biol Chem 1997; 272(21):13829–13834.
- Renault TT, Floros KV, Chipuk JE. BAK/BAX activation and cytochrome c release assays using isolated mitochondria. Methods 2013; 61(2):146–155.
- Renault TT, Floros KV, Elkholi R, Corrigan KA, Kushnareva Y, Wieder SY, Lindtner C, Serasinghe MN, Asciolla JJ, Buettner C, Newmeyer DD, Chipuk JE. Mitochondrial shape governs BAX-induced membrane permeabilization and apoptosis. Mol Cell 2015; 57(1):69–82.
- Chipuk Laboratory received research grants (5-FY11-74 and 1-FY13-416) from the March of Dimes Foundation.

hypothetical_questions.question: What is the purpose of using Trehalose Isolation Buffer (TIB) in the study conducted by the Chipuk Laboratory?
How does the addition of BSA-Fraction V in TIB help eliminate fatty acid and lipid contaminants?
Imagine you are a researcher in the Chipuk Laboratory. How would you design an experiment to study the effect of TIB on BAK/BAX activation?
How does the use of DMSO in the pilot project from NIH P20AA017067 relate to the isolation of liver mitochondria?
How might the findings of the Chipuk Laboratory's study on Real-Time MOMP impact our understanding of mitochondrial function in cancer cells?
